🔬 Active Research

Substrate Effect & Thin-Film Correction

When an indenter penetrates a thin film, the underlying substrate distorts the measured hardness and modulus. This work builds a systematic FEM-based framework to quantify, map, and correct for this error across a wide range of film–substrate combinations.

● In Progress — Jul 2024–Present ● Manuscript in Preparation
1000sFEM simulations run
2Regimes studied
<5%Target correction error

Motivation

Thin-film nanoindentation is used to measure mechanical properties — hardness (H) and elastic modulus (E) — of coatings and surface layers as thin as tens of nanometers. However, when the indentation depth exceeds roughly 10% of the film thickness (the so-called "10% rule"), the measurement is no longer purely representative of the film: the substrate underneath begins to influence the load–displacement response.

This substrate effect leads to systematic over- or under-estimation of material properties depending on whether the substrate is harder or softer than the film. Existing analytical correction models (e.g., Doerner-Nix, Gao, Bec) rely on simplifying assumptions that break down at large indentation depths or for highly mismatched film–substrate systems.

Core problem

The 10% rule is not universally reliable. For very thin films (<200 nm) or extreme property mismatch, significant substrate influence appears well before 10% depth — yet existing correction models fail to capture this. A data-driven correction framework is needed.

Approach

01

Parametric FEM study

Build 2D axisymmetric Abaqus/CAE models covering a broad parameter space: film thickness (50–500 nm), E and H ratios (soft-on-hard and hard-on-soft), indentation depth ratios (h/t = 0.05–0.50), and indenter geometry (spherical and Berkovich-equivalent conical).

02

Sensitivity mapping

For each parameter combination, extract the measured H and E values and compute the percent error relative to the "true" film-only properties. Map these errors as a function of normalized depth h/t across both regimes.

03

ML correction model

Train a neural network surrogate on the FEM dataset to predict the substrate-induced error given observable inputs (indentation depth, load–displacement curve features, estimated modulus mismatch). The model enables real-time correction of experimental measurements.

04

Experimental validation

Validate the correction framework against Cu, Mo, and W thin-film nanoindentation experiments. Compare corrected predictions against reference bulk values to quantify improvement over existing analytical models.

Key findings so far

≫10%
Soft-on-hard error onset — much earlier than the 10% rule predicts
E/H
Mismatch ratio is the dominant driver of substrate influence magnitude
2D
Axisymmetric models validated against 3D — 10× faster at equivalent accuracy
~15%
Maximum observed hardness over-prediction in hard-on-soft at h/t = 0.30
Current focus

Expanding the FEM dataset to cover a wider mismatch ratio range and training the first ML correction model prototype. Target submission: late 2025.

1D-CNN model results

A one-dimensional convolutional neural network was trained to reconstruct bulk load–depth curves from substrate-affected thin-film nanoindentation inputs. The model takes the measured thin-film curve as a sequence and outputs the corresponding corrected bulk response, conditioned on material properties (E, σy, σs) and film thickness. Across the full validation set, the model achieves R² = 0.9857 with near-zero systematic bias (μresidual = −0.028).

Fig. 1 — Training diagnostics. MSE loss curves for the training and validation sets across 23 epochs (linear scale, left; log scale, right). The model converges rapidly within the first 3–4 epochs. Early stopping selected the best checkpoint at epoch 12, where validation loss reaches its minimum before minor fluctuation, confirming no significant overfitting.

Fig. 2 — Parity plot (validation set). Predicted vs. true bulk load across all validation points. The dense clustering along the perfect-prediction line and R² = 0.9857 confirm strong global accuracy. Hexbin density highlights that the majority of predictions fall at low load values where substrate effects are most concentrated.

Fig. 3 — Residual analysis. The residual distribution (left) is well-described by a normal fit with μ = −0.028 and σ = 0.952, indicating negligible systematic bias. The residuals vs. true bulk load plot (right) shows variance is low at small loads and broadens slightly at higher loads, consistent with the depth-dependent error profile.

Fig. 4 — Depth-dependent error profile. Pointwise MSE and MAE computed at each depth increment across the validation set. Error is lowest near the surface (depth ≈ 0) and grows monotonically toward full penetration depth. This reflects the increasing complexity of the substrate-influenced response at deeper indentation, and motivates future work to improve model accuracy in the large-depth regime.

Fig. 5 — Training dataset coverage. Histograms of the three material input parameters: elastic modulus E (mean = 166.6 GPa), yield strength σy (mean = 0.73 GPa), and ultimate strength σs (mean = 1.34 GPa). The broad, roughly uniform distributions confirm that the model was trained across a wide range of material behaviors, supporting generalization beyond any single material system.

Fig. 6 — Individual validation cases (1 µm film thickness). Nine representative examples from the validation set comparing the raw thin-film input curve (gray), the 1D-CNN predicted bulk correction (red dashed), and the true FEM bulk reference (blue). Across all nine cases, R² ranges from 0.950 to 0.998, demonstrating that the model successfully removes substrate-induced distortion and recovers the underlying bulk mechanical response across a diverse range of material properties.

Model summary

Architecture: 1D convolutional neural network · Input: thin-film load–depth sequence + material parameters (E, σy, σs, thickness) · Output: corrected bulk load–depth curve · Global R² = 0.9857 · Residual bias μ = −0.028 · Best epoch: 12 (early stopping)
